name: Fairfax County, Virginia
tagline: Virginia's largest county and a wealthy DC-suburb buyer of scale: ~1.15M residents, an $11B+ all-funds budget, and a well-regarded central IT shop (DIT) that consistently ranks at the top of the Digital Counties Survey.
overview: Fairfax County is the most populous jurisdiction in Virginia (~1.15M residents) and one of the wealthiest large counties in the country, anchoring the Northern Virginia / DC suburbs. It runs a council-manager structure: an elected Board of Supervisors sets policy and a professional County Executive (Bryan Hill) runs day-to-day operations across roughly 50 departments and 200+ facilities. Technology is centralized in the Department of Information Technology (DIT), led by the CIO/DIT Director, which delivers enterprise IT, networks, applications, and cybersecurity for county government (and supports Fairfax County Public Schools on an as-needed basis). DIT is a mature, award-winning shop — Fairfax placed near the top of the Center for Digital Government's Digital Counties Survey in 2025 — making this a sophisticated buyer that values modernization, security, and measurable outcomes over net-new build. For a Dell SLG seller, Fairfax is a large, creditworthy local-government account with its own competitive procurement and active cooperative-contract usage, distinct from (but adjacent to) the Commonwealth's VITA-run state motion.

budget.intro: Fairfax adopted its FY2026 budget at the May 13, 2025 Board of Supervisors meeting. The all-funds plan exceeds $11B, with roughly $5.7B in the General Fund; a large share of the General Fund transfers to the public schools (~$2.93B), so the directly addressable county-operations and IT spend is a subset of the headline figure.

stats.label: General Fund (FY2026)
stats.note: ~$2.93B of which transfers to FCPS schools

stats.label: Technology Infrastructure Services (Fund 60030)
stats.note: FY2026 increase for storage/compute, cloud expansion, and app modernization

notes: Real property tax rate set at $1.1225 per $100 of assessed value for FY2026; property taxes are the dominant revenue source.
Many IT projects are funded through one-time balances and quarterly budget reviews rather than a single line — confirm funding source per opportunity.
A large portion of the General Fund flows to Fairfax County Public Schools, a separate (large) IT buyer worth mapping alongside county DIT.

initiatives.name: IT architecture optimization & infrastructure refresh
initiatives.summary: The FY2026 plan adds ~$5M to Technology Infrastructure Services to expand storage and compute, modernize applications, and continue infrastructure optimization across 50+ departments and 200+ facilities — a direct fit for server, storage, and refresh motions.

initiatives.name: Multi-cloud & cloud governance
initiatives.summary: DIT is pursuing cloud expansion with stronger cloud governance as part of its adaptive technology strategy. Hybrid/multi-cloud and as-a-service offerings are in scope — position Dell infrastructure and managed services around governed cloud, not lift-and-shift.

initiatives.name: Cybersecurity modernization & AI-driven threat detection
initiatives.summary: The Information Security Office is building an adaptive security strategy aligned to AI, IoT, and multi-cloud — upgrading security infrastructure, deploying AI-assisted threat detection, and automating operations. Fairfax's security program is nationally recognized and a priority spend area.

initiatives.name: AI adoption & responsible-use governance
initiatives.summary: County leadership is moving on generative AI — countywide responsible-use training/security guidance plus a regional AI Economy Action Plan (estimated $0.5M–$1.5M with the NoVA Regional Commission). AI is both an internal operations lever (Hill has cited workforce/vacancy pressure) and an economic-development theme.

procurement.intro: Fairfax runs its own competitive procurement through the Department of Procurement and Material Management and is an active cooperative-contract user — both regionally and nationally. Plan for either a county solicitation or a ride on an existing cooperative vehicle; confirm Dell's coverage on the relevant ones before forecasting.

vehicles.name: Fairfax County direct procurement
vehicles.detail: County-run competitive solicitations via Procurement and Material Management, including a standing IT Hardware, Software, and Associated Services contract serving 50+ departments. Check the county Contract Register for current awards.

vehicles.name: Cooperative contracts (OMNIA Partners / national co-ops)
vehicles.detail: Fairfax solicits, awards, and rides national cooperative contracts (OMNIA Partners and successor programs to US Communities). A pre-competed co-op can shorten the path to award.

vehicles.name: Metropolitan Washington COG cooperative
vehicles.detail: Regionally, Fairfax uses Metropolitan Washington Council of Governments cooperative contracts and offers vendors a COG rider at award — a route to serve multiple NoVA jurisdictions.

vehicles.name: Virginia statewide (VITA) contracts
vehicles.detail: As a Virginia public body, Fairfax may leverage Commonwealth/VITA-established vehicles where eligible — useful as an alternate path, though the county largely runs its own buys.

powerStructure.name: Bryan J. Hill
powerStructure.title: County Executive
powerStructure.note: Top professional administrator running ~50 departments; AI-forward (NACo AI Exploratory Committee) and has cited AI to address an ~18% vacancy rate. Sets enterprise direction.

powerStructure.name: Matt Harrison
powerStructure.title: CIO & Director, Dept of Information Technology (DIT)
powerStructure.note: Appointed March 2025, started April 21, 2025, succeeding longtime CIO Greg Scott. Background: Stafford County IT Director and GEICO Technology Solutions (data/AI). The top IT relationship.

powerStructure.name: Charlie Gore
powerStructure.title: CISO & DIT Deputy Director
powerStructure.note: Leads the Information Security Office; owns security policy, incident response, and enterprise security tooling. Succeeded longtime CISO Michael Dent (now Strategic Liaison to CIO/CISO since Aug 2025).

powerStructure.name: Dept of Procurement & Material Management
powerStructure.title: County procurement authority
powerStructure.note: Owns solicitations, awards, and cooperative-contract decisions — the gatekeeper for any county purchase and the path onto county or co-op vehicles.

powerStructure.name: Board of Supervisors
powerStructure.title: Elected governing body
powerStructure.note: Adopts the budget and sets tax rates and policy; approves major contracts. The political layer above the County Executive.

news.title: County pursues regional AI Economy Action Plan (with NoVA Regional Commission)
news.impact: Estimated $0.5M–$1.5M effort signals AI as both an internal-ops and economic-development priority — an opening for AI-infrastructure and advisory conversations.

news.title: Longtime CISO Michael Dent moves to Strategic Liaison role; Charlie Gore leads ISO
news.impact: Security leadership succession completed — re-confirm the CISO relationship and current security roadmap owners.

news.title: Board adopts FY2026 budget ($11B+ all funds; $1.1225 tax rate)
news.impact: Tax-rate increase and targeted IT/infrastructure investments funded — confirms budget headroom for refresh, cloud, and security spend.

news.title: Matt Harrison starts as CIO/DIT Director (succeeding Greg Scott)
news.impact: New IT leadership with cloud/AI and Microsoft-modernization track record — fresh agenda; time relationship-building and roadmap discovery early.

dellActions: Map the buying centers: DIT (Harrison) for enterprise IT/infrastructure, the ISO (Gore) for security, and Procurement & Material Management for the path to award — and treat FCPS as a separate adjacent IT buyer.
Lead infrastructure with the FY2026 architecture-optimization and ~$5M Technology Infrastructure Services investment — storage/compute expansion, app modernization, and governed cloud.
Confirm Dell coverage on the vehicles Fairfax actually uses (county IT hardware/software/services contract, OMNIA Partners, COG co-op) to shorten time-to-award.
Engage the security modernization and AI agenda: AI-driven threat detection, multi-cloud governance, and responsible-AI infrastructure align with Dell ISG/AI-factory positioning.
Use Fairfax's Digital Counties standing and the regional AI Economy Action Plan as door-openers for executive (Hill/Harrison) modernization and AI conversations.
